ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken curry' class (FOODON_00002809); no specific Thai green curry subclass exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Thai green curry code. A0CKP (chicken meat dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai green curry chicken as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry chicken with steamed jasmine rice, approximately 400 g total, comprising roughly 200 g curry and 200 g cooked rice.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Green Curry with Chicken — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for Thai green curry chicken set exists. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for chicken (cooked, boneless), coconut milk, and green curry paste components, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data for similar Southeast Asian coconut-based chicken curry dishes. Per-100g values represent the curry portion only (excluding rice). Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed coconut milk-based chicken curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for coconut milk curry and chicken-based mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai green curry chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous coconut milk-based curry dishes reported in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021). The high fat and protein content from coconut milk and chicken, combined with low carbohydrate density, supports a low GI classification. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 16.8) / 100 ≈ 6.4, where carbohydrate_g per serving is based on 400 g total serving with approximately 200 g curry portion contributing ~8.4 g carbohydrate from curry plus ~8.4 g from rice portion estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and curry paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a standard 200 g curry serving contributes approximately 760 mg sodium, representing over 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index due to high fat and protein content from coconut milk and chicken
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai green curry chicken has a low estimated GI of approximately 38, making it a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided the accompanying rice portion is controlled and total carbohydrate intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content (approximately 4.9 g per 100 g) derived primarily from coconut milk warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as regular consumption of saturated fat is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol; portion control and frequency limitation are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai green curry chicken (แกงเขียวหวานไก่, Kaeng Khiao Wan Kai) is a classic Thai dish consisting of tender chicken pieces simmered in a rich, aromatic green curry sauce made from coconut milk, green curry paste, Thai basil, kaffir lime leaves, lemongrass, galangal, fish sauce, and palm sugar. The curry paste is typically prepared from fresh green chillies, shallots, garlic, galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime zest, coriander root, cumin, and shrimp paste, giving the dish its characteristic vibrant green colour and complex layered flavour profile. The dish is simultaneously spicy, creamy, slightly sweet, and savoury, with a smooth coconut milk base that tempers the heat of the chillies. Thai eggplant and bamboo shoots are commonly added for texture. In Singapore, it is widely served as a set meal with steamed jasmine rice at Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, the curry portion is moderate in calories (approximately 112 kcal per 100 g), high in protein from chicken, and contains significant saturated fat from coconut milk. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and shrimp paste. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 38, making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when rice portions are controlled. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium load for hypertensive individuals and saturated fat intake for those with cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Thai green curry chicken set found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data for analogous Southeast Asian coconut-based chicken curry dishes. GI estimated from analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and cross-referenced across two approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the curry portion per 100 g, excluding the rice component of the set meal. The full set meal (curry + rice) would have a different nutritional profile. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA or FAO/INFOODS source data for this dish type. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish type. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA ingredient-level data for coconut milk and chicken — no industrial trans fats are present in traditional Thai green curry preparation. 5. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific dish. 6. Halal status is marked false because traditional green curry paste contains shrimp paste (kapi), which may not be halal-certified; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified independently. 7. The input record listed cuisine_tag 'Pad Thai' which appears to be a data error from the field survey source; this tag has been disregarded as it is inconsistent with the food identity.
